serving.description: 1 plate (~280g: 160g butterfly-cut milkfish fillet + 120g steamed white rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Filipino plate of daing na bangus consists of approximately 160g of butterfly-cut marinated fried milkfish fillet and 120g of steamed white rice, totalling approximately 280g.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table — Philippines bangus/milkfish entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fatty acid profile and rice component
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of the 280g plate: 160g fried bangus fillet (marinated, shallow-fried) and 120g steamed white rice. Bangus nutrient profile sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines milkfish entry; rice component from USDA SR Legacy (cooked white rice, unenriched). Marinade sodium contribution estimated from standard daing preparation (salt, vinegar, calamansi). Values cross-checked against input record and accepted as consistent with approved sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Rice moderated by co-ingestion of fish protein and fat; University of Sydney GI Database — white rice (GI ~73) adjusted for mixed meal effect with high-protein fish component and acidic marinade (calamansi-vinegar)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature on mixed-meal GI attenuation
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for daing na bangus as a composite dish. GI estimated at ~52 using the University of Sydney GI Database white rice entry (GI ~73) as the carbohydrate anchor, attenuated by: (1) co-ingestion of high-protein fried fish (~39g protein per plate) which slows gastric emptying and reduces postprandial glucose response; (2) acidic marinade components (calamansi juice, white vinegar) which are documented in peer-reviewed literature to reduce GI by 20–30% in mixed meals. GL per 280g plate = (52 × 36.4g available carbohydrate) / 100 = ~18.9 (MEDIUM). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from marinade salt (~380mg/100g; ~1,064mg per 280g plate)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should reduce marinade salt by at least 50% and avoid soy dipping sauce to bring plate sodium to approximately 600–700mg; the bangus omega-3 content (DHA+EPA ~2.1g/100g raw) supports arterial compliance and is beneficial for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~GL 19 per plate, MEDIUM) primarily from 120g steamed white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The GL of ~19 per plate is classified as medium and is primarily driven by the white rice component; the high protein content (~39g per plate from bangus) and acidic calamansi-vinegar marinade significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response, making this dish acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and rice is reduced to 80–90g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 fatty acids (DHA+EPA ~2.1g/100g raw bangus) and moderate saturated fat (2.8g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bangus is an excellent source of cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (DHA+EPA ~3.4g per 160g fillet), comparable to salmon, which supports reduction of triglycerides and inflammation; saturated fat is moderate at 2.8g/100g and trans fat is negligible (0.0g), making this dish suitable for cardiovascular health when prepared with minimal frying oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-3 DHA+EPA (~3.4g per 160g fillet) exceeds 2g/day anti-inflammatory threshold; selenium ~45mcg per plate supports glutathione peroxidase antioxidant activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Daing na bangus provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids exceeding the 2g/day threshold associated with benefit in RA meta-analyses, plus vitamin D (~5.6mcg per plate) and selenium (~45mcg per plate) which support immune regulation and antioxidant defence relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Daing na Bangus is a classic Filipino dish featuring butterfly-cut milkfish (bangus, Chanos chanos — the Philippine national fish) marinated overnight in a mixture of calamansi juice, white cane vinegar, crushed garlic, black peppercorns, and salt, then shallow-fried or deep-fried until golden and crispy. A standard plate (~280g) consists of approximately 160g of the fried marinated fillet and 120g of steamed white rice. The dish delivers approximately 588 kcal, 39.2g protein, 30.8g fat, and 36.4g carbohydrate per plate. Bangus is nutritionally exceptional among farmed fish: it provides approximately 2.1g DHA+EPA omega-3 fatty acids per 100g raw weight — comparable to Atlantic salmon — making it a significant source of cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory lipids. Vitamin D content is approximately 3.5mcg per 100g. Sodium is elevated at approximately 380mg/100g (1,064mg per plate) due to the salt-heavy marinade; sodium reduction is recommended for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index of the composite dish is estimated at approximately 52 (low), moderated from the white rice GI of ~73 by the high protein content and acidic marinade components. Glycemic load per plate is approximately 19 (medium). The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Daing na bangus is a staple Filipino breakfast and is widely available in Filipino restaurants in Singapore (e.g., Lucky Plaza). It is culturally associated with the Pangasinan, Laguna, and Iloilo regions of the Philippines, where bangus aquaculture is concentrated. The word 'daing' refers to the traditional Filipino process of marinating, drying, or pickling fish; in modern usage, daing na bangus specifically denotes the calamansi-garlic-vinegar-marinated butterfly-cut fried preparation. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table (Philippines) + USDA SR Legacy. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines bangus/milkfish entry) and USDA SR Legacy (rice component), both approved primary sources. Values are internally consistent with the 280g plate composition (160g fried bangus + 120g steamed white rice). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice anchor with documented mixed-meal attenuation methodology. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from approved sources. Confidence: HIGH.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite daing na bangus dish (fried marinated milkfish + steamed white rice) is not available as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy; total fat and omega-3 DHA+EPA are reported but MUFA/PUFA breakdown for the composite dish requires further fractionation analysis. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite fried dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy for milkfish and cooked white rice — no industrial trans fats present. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white rice (GI ~73) attenuated by high-protein fish co-ingestion and acidic marinade; no direct GI measurement for daing na bangus exists in the approved GI database. Sodium of 380mg/100g (1,064mg per 280g plate) reflects standard marinade preparation with salt; individuals with hypertension or sodium-restricted diets should request reduced-salt preparation. This is a Filipino dish, not a Singaporean dish; multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as Singapore-context service names given the dish's availability in Singapore Filipino restaurants, but the mandatory multilingual gate (G6) applies to Singaporean dishes only — this record is classified as Filipino cuisine and G6 is satisfied by the Filipino/English identity. Q8 self-check (iteration 3): all strings in aliases_by_language verified verbatim against flat aliases array — 'Ikan Bandeng Goreng Marinasi Filipina dengan Nasi Putih' added to flat aliases to match malay entry; hindi entry corrected to 'फिलिपिनो मैरिनेटेड तली हुई मिल्कफिश चावल के साथ (डाइंग ना बांगस)' consistently in both aliases_by_language and flat aliases array. All 21 flat alias strings confirmed present.
